main_summary.summary: Rick Tuttle, interviewed in Culver City, California on April 11, 2013 by David Cline for the Southern Oral History Program with the Smithsonian NMAAHC and Library of Congress, traces a civil rights trajectory from a New England Civil War family lineage and an early childhood encounter with NAACP-organized Pullman porters through fraternity desegregation at Wesleyan University, graduate study at UCLA, and direct participation in SNCC and SCLC voter-registration and desegregation work in 1963. Recruited by Steve McNichols after reading a John Poppy article on Sam Block, Charles McLaurin, and Willie Wazir Peacock, Tuttle worked the COFO office in Greenwood, Mississippi during the murder of Medgar Evers and the Winona jailing of Fannie Lou Hamer, Lawrence Guyot, June Johnson, and Annelle Ponder. He was reassigned to Atlanta, then to Savannah with Hosea Williams and Benjamin Van Clark, where he was arrested under Judge Victor Mulling's seven-piece-bond detention and held in the Chatham County Jail for roughly six weeks until attorney Aaron Buchsbaum and Dr. William Waxler secured his release. Subsequent work included the UCLA Student Coordinating Committee, the No on Proposition 14 fair-housing campaign, the RFK 1968 campaign, the Tom Bradley 1973 mayoral coalition, four terms as Los Angeles City Controller, private-club desegregation, South Africa divestment, and redlining disclosure ordinances.
key_themes: SNCC and SCLC field organizing in Mississippi and Georgia 1963
White ally jail risk and the institutional protection of federal-family ties
Cross-coalition liberal-progressive electoral politics in Los Angeles
Continuity from civil rights work to fair-housing, anti-apartheid, and gender/orientation equity
Voting Rights Act preservation and the long arc of movement institutions
main_summary.historical_significance:
The interview is a primary-source account of the COFO Greenwood office during the exact week of the Medgar Evers assassination and the Winona jail beatings, with Tuttle's direct phone call to Governor Ross Barnett and his attribution of that conversation to the eventual release of Hamer, Guyot, Ponder, and June Johnson. It documents the Savannah movement under Hosea Williams and Benjamin Van Clark from the position of a jailed white volunteer, including the Chatham County Jail trustees who physically protected Tuttle, the Aaron Buchsbaum bail intervention, and the suppressed Atlanta Constitution communist-smear story that was killed by Stanley Mosk's letter and Frank Cherry's call to the publisher.

It also traces continuity from the 1963 Mississippi-Georgia experience to the UCLA Student Coordinating Committee, the No on Proposition 14 referendum, the RFK 1968 California campaign, the Tom Bradley 1973 mayoral coalition, and Tuttle's four terms as Los Angeles City Controller, during which he led the private-club desegregation ordinance and the South Africa divestment affidavit requirement. The 2008 return tour with his second wife Rebecca to Greenwood, Canton, and Savannah adds reflective coda material on the persistence of Delta poverty and the institutional memory of the movement.

chapters.part: A Family Raised on the War
chapters.title: He Died on the Petersburg Front
chapters.summary: Interviewed in Culver City, California, on April 11, 2013, by David Cline with videographer John Bishop, Rick Tuttle opens with his birth in New Haven into a New England family settled there since 1638, and the family story of his grandmother's grandfather, who in the assault wave after the Massachusetts 54th was cut down at Fort Wagner near Charleston and later died on the Petersburg front, reflecting on Lincoln's refusal of a Confederate peace to abolish slavery.

chapters.part: A Family Raised on the War
chapters.title: The Clatter of Dishes at the Table
chapters.summary: Tuttle describes being the eldest grandchild raised on clashing Civil War memories, his father's Connecticut mother and his mother's Arkansas mother who told bitter stories of torture when the Union army came through, the two grandmothers unable even to agree whether to call it the Civil War or the war between the states, sometimes storming away from the dinner table, and his own father going into the Marine Corps at thirty-six.

chapters.part: A Family Raised on the War
chapters.title: The Card Was the NAACP
chapters.summary: He remembers traveling as a small boy with his pregnant mother and brothers from Jonesboro, Arkansas, north through Memphis and Cincinnati in 1944-45 on jammed wartime trains, and how, with the New York connection about to pull out across distant tracks, his father appealed to the Black sleeping-car porter, showed him a card, and the porters held the train; the card was his father's NAACP membership, his first lesson in what organization can do.

chapters.part: A Family Raised on the War
chapters.title: The Finest Track Coach in New England
chapters.summary: Tuttle describes his father, a great athlete, Marine, and high school track coach who won nine of ten Connecticut state championships and coached many young Black runners, later a principal at a predominantly African American school in the Dixwell Avenue area of New Haven, civically engaged though not electorally political, and his own move through ten schools to Plattsburgh High and Wesleyan University.

chapters.title: We Gave Them One Year, Then We Fired Them
chapters.summary: He recounts joining the Alpha Chi Rho fraternity at Wesleyan, where his chapter had found a clause requiring allegiance to Jesus Christ and, by report, a hidden whites-only clause, becoming house president and leading the multi-year battle to keep the chapter's property while severing from the national, giving them one year to change and, when they refused, cutting them loose, a fight that taught him the vocabulary of civil rights.

chapters.title: Little Rock and a Call of Conscience
chapters.summary: Tuttle recalls Little Rock unfolding in the fall of 1957, his settled conviction that segregation was a bad idea, harmful and crippling for whites, and the Wesleyan religion instructor John Maguire, a Yale Divinity man from Alabama who had marched with Martin Luther King and, after the Anniston bus burning, declared a call of conscience to join the Freedom Ride.

chapters.title: Wesleyan Went on the Freedom Ride
chapters.summary: He describes department chair David Swift, on hearing Maguire's call of conscience, telephoning his wife to pack his bags and joining the Freedom Ride himself, and how Tuttle and his roommate, primed enough to want to go after Anniston, were not operational and missed the bus that pulled out before they could get there.

chapters.title: The Genius of the Freedom Rides
chapters.summary: Tuttle lays out the genius of the Freedom Rides as interstate travel that forced the commerce-law issue, recalls earlier 1940s efforts out of Cleveland, his graduation in 1962, and credits fraternity brothers Stephen S. Trott and Bruce Corwin, who had forced the desegregation issue by refusing to join if the clause affected anyone.

chapters.title: I Came 3,000 Miles to Work With You
chapters.summary: Tuttle recounts coming west to Los Angeles in 1962 for sunshine, basketball, and graduate study in history at UCLA, marching into the office hours of progressive-movement historian George Mowry, who had been close to Frank Graham at Chapel Hill, to win a place in his full seminar.

chapters.title: West Coast Riders and a Familiar Face
chapters.summary: He describes defending the National Student Association in the UCLA Daily Bruin, meeting Steve McNichols who became the NSA representative, a circle of West Coast Freedom Riders including Bob Singleton, Helen Bailey Singleton, and Robert Farrell, the Singletons jailed at Parchman and McNichols beaten on the Houston ride, and McNichols bringing speakers including Chuck McDew of SNCC and Malcolm X, whom Tuttle had hosted at his Wesleyan fraternity and who laughed to find him following along as a graduate student.

chapters.title: All Hell Broke Loose in Birmingham
chapters.summary: He recalls reading John Poppy's magazine account of Sam Block, Charles McLaurin, and Willie Wazir Peacock registering voters in the Mississippi Delta near where Emmett Till was killed, being moved by their harrowing experiences, watching the Birmingham fire hoses in May 1963 while serving as a weekend nanny for the family of Norman Raskin, and Raskin encouraging him over graham crackers and milk to go if he ever could.

chapters.title: My Man, It Looks Like You're Going to Mississippi
chapters.summary: Tuttle describes weighing the choice with no debt and no spouse, bumping into McNichols at the Kerckhoff Hall mailbox, who said Sam Block and Charles McLaurin would be at the campus religious center that night and could arrange a ride, the decision sealed when a young woman walked up and Steve announced Rick was going to Mississippi to help SNCC.

chapters.title: Some People You Need to Meet
chapters.summary: He recounts the civil rights conference at the campus religious center where Block and McLaurin told their story, and McNichols taking him with graduate student Paul Albert to a Hollywood Hills party celebrating the election of Congressman George E. Brown Jr and city councilman Tom Bradley, hosted around the circle Californians for Liberal Representation.

chapters.title: A Letter on Congressional Letterhead
chapters.summary: Tuttle explains the plan for Congressman George E. Brown Jr to write him a letter on official letterhead saying he had been asked to observe voter registration practices in the South, meant not to sway officials but to give pause to private attackers who might hesitate if they thought him federal, and his call home where his mother begged, honey, not Mississippi, before he drove to Greenwood with Block and McLaurin.

chapters.title: Go Down Moses, Way Down in Freedom Land
chapters.topic: Church and Rally in the Delta
chapters.summary: He describes arriving in Greenwood with the organizers Sam Block and Charles McLaurin, hearing in a small rural church the Exodus story preached with powerful linkages between the Hebrew people and Black Mississippians, and a voter registration rally on a Canton baseball field in 1963 where Willie Wazir Peacock told him it was time to go as carloads of men with Confederate flags gathered at dusk.

chapters.title: It Was a COFO Operation
chapters.summary: Tuttle clarifies that the Greenwood work was the Council of Federated Organizations, COFO, joining SNCC, NAACP, CORE, and SCLC at the office at 708 Avenue N, and describes sleeping in the same bed as Lawrence Guyot, who one Sunday morning rose to bail out Fannie Lou Hamer and young women arrested in Winona, only to be handcuffed and beaten with baseball bats and jailed.

chapters.title: The Bureau Was More Interested in Communists
chapters.summary: He describes Willie Wazir Peacock taking charge of the office, the near-absence of friendly law enforcement in 1963 Mississippi with the local FBI more interested in communists than civil rights, and Peacock having him telephone Julian Bond and reporters Claude Sitton of the New York Times and Karl Fleming of Newsweek, then call Medgar Evers about bail money just as Evers was leaving for a meeting.

chapters.title: By Randela Back With Murder
chapters.summary: Tuttle recounts that Medgar Evers was murdered that night before he could call back, all hell breaking loose in Jackson and John Doar walking down the street holding up his Justice Department credentials, and the desperate fear that Hamer and the young women, including June Johnson, could not be found and might be moved around in danger as people turned up dead and missing across segregated Mississippi.

chapters.title: Are You Threatening Me, Son?
chapters.summary: He describes being scripted by Willie Wazir Peacock to telephone the governor, identifying himself as a graduate student from California helping the civil rights effort, and warning that unless Hamer, Guyot, Annelle Ponder, June Johnson, and the others were located and released there would be major demonstrations in the Delta; the governor asked if he was threatening him, and Tuttle insisted he was only reporting.

chapters.title: He Was Going to Kill the White Boy
chapters.summary: Tuttle recalls the highway patrol revealing before the deadline that the prisoners were at the Winona jail where they could be bailed out, Andrew Young driving in with bail money through the valley of the shadow of death, and word that Byron De La Beckwith, the Greenwood man who had killed Medgar Evers, meant to kill the white boy, so they hid Tuttle under a blanket to fly him to the SNCC office in Atlanta.

chapters.title: Lester Maddox With a Pickaxe Handle
chapters.summary: Flown to Memphis and on to the Atlanta SNCC office, which pushed past a settlement that had cooled lunch-counter activity, Tuttle joined a group walking to desegregate a counter, passing within clear sight of Lester Maddox gesturing in front of his restaurant, and sat in as an upset waitress stood over him holding a pot of boiling water with Black and white demonstrators seated together.

chapters.title: You Folks Down to Savannah
chapters.summary: He recounts the tense sit-in ending when a police officer called the waitress by name to settle down, reporters behind them including John Kifner, a Wesleyan man covering the movement for the Berkshire Eagle, and returning to the Atlanta SNCC office where Casey Hayden and Dion Diamond asked him, Bruce Gordon, and Haverford student Joe Iyer to drive to Savannah to support the Chatham County Crusade for Voters.

chapters.title: Spellbinding Oratory in a Packed Church
chapters.summary: Tuttle recalls arriving at night to a packed Savannah church where the Chatham County Crusade for Voters, led by Hosea Williams and Benjamin Van Clark, had mobilized major night marches for desegregation of public facilities, hotels, and employment, and the spellbinding oratory of Hosea Williams, a wounded World War II veteran and government chemist.

chapters.title: Put Your Things in These Baskets
chapters.summary: He describes the scene before each night march, when leaders asked everyone to put weapons in baskets so nothing could discredit them, the clatter of brass knuckles and switchblades as people gave up what they normally carried for self-defense, and how the marchers stayed upstairs in a house of prostitution whose owners supported the movement pro bono and even put up bail money.

chapters.title: Eyes and Ears at the Meetings
chapters.summary: Tuttle recounts the daring assignment Benjamin Van Clark gave him, to move to separate quarters away from the cameras of the marches and attend white supremacist public meetings on the outskirts of Savannah as eyes and ears, where police officers were introduced as friends, spending his days at a single movie and the library before slipping out to the gatherings.

chapters.title: A Building Burns in Savannah
chapters.summary: He describes being called back to the movement office, John Kifner arriving to put away his reporter's notebook and join the effort, and a night when he happened to be sick as a fire broke out and a building burned with the leadership being jailed, leaving Savannah's structure leaderless, until Andrew Young and James Bevel came to the office.

chapters.title: They Came for John Doe, White
chapters.summary: Tuttle recounts Savannah police entering the Chatham County Crusade for Voters office to arrest him, his realizing his one assignment was to keep them from noticing Andrew Young and James Bevel as Young eased away, the warrant that named not Rick Tuttle but John Doe, white, and being cuffed and led past a United Press reporter into solitary confinement, where a disembodied voice in the dark repeated that the man was not in the Klan but his brother was, on a night the Klan was marching in Savannah.

chapters.title: Seven Peace Bonds Apiece
chapters.summary: Tuttle describes being brought to court with some forty-five civil rights people including Hosea Williams, represented by James Nabrit III and advised to give unsworn testimony, before Judge Victor Mullen, a blind segregationist of the Talmadge faction who revived stacked peace bonds, his own seven, each requiring unencumbered property no one could supply, while the complainant against him could not identify him in the courtroom.

chapters.title: You Leave This Guy Alone
chapters.summary: He recounts a Ronald Goldfarb lecture at Yale Law that later cited the onerous bail case, being moved into the white cell block across a wire screen from the Black male prisoners and Bruce Gordon, and staying fit with constant push-ups so the car-theft and drunk-driving inmates never touched him.

chapters.title: The Trustees Put It on the Line
chapters.summary: Tuttle tells the story of the jail trustees, the bridge boys including a welterweight boxer and longshoreman known as Felian Popeye, who warned the white prisoners during strip searches to leave him alone or be beaten, risking their easy time and a transfer to hard time at a state prison farm for a relative stranger they accepted as a civil rights worker.

chapters.title: We're Going to Run a Freedom School
chapters.summary: He describes how he and Bruce Gordon, refusing to behave like criminals, started a freedom school in the jail with Tuttle teaching history, Gordon theater arts, and someone else mathematics in clusters through the day, the visible effect on the guards, and his parents working to represent him to a judge who took him for someone big-time.

chapters.title: He Shows Up to Bless Me
chapters.summary: Tuttle recounts the allies who answered, the Episcopal campus priest Father Crowder, who had raised money for his trip and battled segregationist fraternities and was later Bishop of Kimberley expelled by apartheid, stopping on his way to a Toronto conference to give him communion in jail, and aides sent down by Congressman George E. Brown Jr.

chapters.title: The Fifth Circuit and a Familiar Name
chapters.topic: A Tangle of Bail and Courts
chapters.summary: He describes sitting in custody under tough bail terms during a summer bracketed by Birmingham and the March on Washington and the church bombing that killed the children, hopes of relief from a Fifth Circuit hesitant amid Senator Eastland's threat to split it, and the presiding judge Elbert Parr Tuttle sharing his surname though no relation, while his NASA-employed father drew minor press notice.

chapters.title: A Stranger in a Strange Land
chapters.summary: Tuttle reflects that white volunteers were meant to do as told and, by drawing fire, draw attention to the cause, and traces how word reached New Haven, where the Anti-Defamation League's Izzy Waxler, a fellow World War II-era family man close to his father, used his contacts to reach the ADL network in Atlanta and Savannah, leading toward attorney Aaron Buchsbaum.

chapters.title: The Rise and Fall of the Third Reich
chapters.summary: He describes how parallel tracks, the ADL contacts and his Harvard Law friend Stephen S. Trott, both led to Savannah attorney Aaron Buchsbaum, who agreed to represent him, and Dr. William Waxler, a former alderman and Buchsbaum's relative by marriage, who put up his property, and how Buchsbaum brought him that book just as a story moved across the Atlanta Constitution that the Crusade for Voters office was rented from the Mine, Mill, and Smelter Workers, expelled from the CIO in 1947 as communist-controlled, a story both men saw was aimed at him.

chapters.title: Rick Tuttle Is Not a Communist
chapters.summary: He describes his father reaching the Corwin family, Victor Carter, and Sherrill Corwin, who reached California Attorney General Stanley Mosk, Mosk writing the Georgia attorney general with copy to the Atlanta Constitution to vouch that Rick Tuttle was no communist, and his mother reaching a girlfriend married to former Arkansas governor Frank Cherry, head of the federal Subversive Activities Control Board, who phoned the publisher to say knock it off, so the story died; Tuttle reflects he had brought a considerable constellation of forces to the table, perhaps part of the insight behind bringing volunteers for Freedom Summer.

chapters.title: The First City in the South to Desegregate
chapters.summary: He describes Buchsbaum and Waxler taking the case to another Savannah judge who ordered his release on a single property bond, the original charge of seven peace bonds for disturbing the peace, his word to leave Savannah so everyone could get out, and that through a variety of techniques Savannah became, before the Civil Rights Act, the first city in the South to desegregate under moderate mayor Malcolm Maclean.

chapters.title: We Could Hear the March on Washington
chapters.summary: Tuttle recounts being in custody for a month and a half, the freedom schools and the March on Washington heard on the upstairs television prompting a perceptible shift even among hard-line guards toward recognition that the civil rights side might win, and reflects again that Medgar Evers in death was helpful in securing the Winona prisoners' release.

chapters.title: The Networks That Built the March
chapters.summary: He describes organizing crowds in Atlanta to gather as Medgar Evers' funeral train came through, his belief that the very relationships built to assemble people along that route were shortly redeployed to carpool, bus, and train people into Washington for the March, and his intention to write Mrs. Evers as the fiftieth anniversary approaches before returning to California.

chapters.title: Not the Man Who Tells the Stories
chapters.summary: Tuttle says one of his better decisions was not to become someone endlessly telling these stories, recounts marrying Rebecca and revisiting the civil rights sites in 2008, Winona, Greenwood, Canton, and Savannah, where he saw his old jail cell, the Hosea Williams collection in Atlanta, and a Telfair Museum exhibit quoting him, and a moving return to 708 Avenue N in Greenwood, where a woman who had been around the COFO office as a teenager recognized them and the group broke into song as wary young men came back in.

chapters.title: No on Proposition 14
chapters.topic: Fair Housing and Friends of SNCC
chapters.summary: Back in 1963 California, Tuttle describes forming a student coordinating committee at UCLA co-chaired with Bob Singleton and Russ Ellis, mobilizing over a hundred students each weekend and nine hundred on Election Day in the losing fight against Proposition 14 for fair housing, and serving as a Friends of SNCC group lobbying Congressmen Gus Hawkins, Phil Burton, and William Fitts Ryan to hold hearings in Mississippi.

chapters.title: Five Assassinations in Five Years
chapters.summary: He traces the connection to the Berkeley Free Speech Movement and Jack Weinberg, the James Jones Board of Education campaign leading into Tom Bradley's mayoral runs, meeting his first wife in Robert Kennedy's campaign where they ran into John Lewis, the toll of five assassinations in less than five years from Medgar Evers through Robert Kennedy, and his conviction that there was a conspiracy to kill King.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr

chapters.title: Standing on the Shoulders of the Turnout
chapters.topic: City Controller and the Private Clubs
chapters.summary: Tuttle describes how Bradley, seeing the African American and Latino turnout achieved with United Farm Workers help in 1968, won the mayoralty on the second try, his own service as a UCLA administrator, the LA Community College Board, and four terms as Los Angeles City Controller, where he led the way to an ordinance barring discrimination at clubs like the Jonathan Club and California Club.

chapters.title: Nothing Like a Sworn Affidavit
chapters.summary: He recounts pushing the private-club desegregation hard despite resistance, then turning as controller to the anti-apartheid fight, winning a battle with Zev Yaroslavsky and Robert Farrell to make Los Angeles divest from companies doing business in South Africa, using sworn affidavits to force firms that had lied or chosen not to know about their ties to disclose them.

chapters.title: These Battles Go On
chapters.summary: Tuttle mentions redlining-disclosure ordinances pursued as controller, a resolution he would carry to the California Democratic Party urging the Supreme Court not to reverse the 1965 Voting Rights Act and its federal preclearance, and his closing reflection that the voting-rights battle naturally flowed into gender equity and rights for gay, lesbian, and transgender Americans, before David Cline thanks him and the interview ends.

loc_video.caption: Rick Tuttle oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in Culver City, California, 2013 April 11
